title: Stack and queue applications

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Stack and queue applications; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Stack and queue applications
sources.claim: Stacks, queues, and deques are selected from the ordering discipline required by the algorithm, not from surface similarity.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Stacks, queues, and deques are selected from the ordering discipline required by the algorithm, not from surface similarity.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Breadth-first search uses a queue while depth-first iterative traversal uses a stack. Your goal is to implement a delimiter checker and one queue-driven simulation with tests. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Linked lists; Stack ADT; Queue and circular buffer. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Stacks, queues, and deques are selected from the ordering discipline required by the algorithm, not from surface similarity. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: The same problem can require multiple structures, and language library adaptors expose different method names and failure rules. The draft therefore rejects: Stack and queue can be exchanged without changing traversal order.

includes: Apply stacks to nesting or evaluation and queues to buffering or breadth-first work.
Implement a delimiter checker and one queue-driven simulation with tests.

In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
excludes: The same problem can require multiple structures, and language library adaptors expose different method names and failure rules.

outcomes: Implement a delimiter checker and one queue-driven simulation with tests.

examples.title: Stack and queue applications: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Breadth-first search uses a queue while depth-first iterative traversal uses a stack.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Breadth-first search uses a queue while depth-first iterative traversal uses a stack.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Choose LIFO for deferred most-recent work and FIFO for arrival-order work; the choice changes observable traversal.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Choose LIFO for deferred most-recent work and FIFO for arrival-order work; the choice changes observable traversal.

examples.title: Stack and queue applications: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: The same problem can require multiple structures, and language library adaptors expose different method names and failure rules.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Stack and queue can be exchanged without changing traversal order.
misconceptions.correction: Choose LIFO for deferred most-recent work and FIFO for arrival-order work; the choice changes observable traversal.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Stack and queue can be exchanged without changing traversal order.
